   148‚Üí- [ ] **Empty state**: When the user has no todos, show a brief, friendly message
   149‚Üí  (e.g., "No todos yet.") -- not a blank screen. The add-todo input above makes the
   150‚Üí  next action obvious; don't over-explain.
   151‚Üí- [ ] Page title (`<title>`) is descriptive (e.g., "My Todos -- Todo List")
   152‚Üí- [ ] The page includes proper landmark regions (`<main>`, `<nav>`, `<header>`)
   153‚Üí- [ ] Skip link to main content is the first focusable element
   154‚Üí- [ ] Each user can only see their own todos (authorization check)
   155‚Üí- [ ] Page works without JavaScript
   156‚Üí
   157‚Üí### Edge Cases
   158‚Üí
   159‚Üí- User with zero todos (empty state)
   160‚Üí- User with one todo
   161‚Üí- User with many todos (100+) -- still performant and usable
   162‚Üí- Todo titles with special characters, HTML entities, or very long text
   163‚Üí- Accessing another user's todo list URL (must return 403 or redirect)
   164‚Üí
   165‚Üí### Success Metric
   166‚Üí
   167‚Üí- A user can see their entire todo list within 1 second of logging in. The list is
   168‚Üí  scannable -- the user's eye goes directly to what needs doing.
   169‚Üí
   170‚Üí---
   171‚Üí
   172‚Üí## US-5: Add a Todo Item (Must Have)
   173‚Üí
   174‚Üí**As a** logged-in user,
   175‚Üí**I want to** add a new todo item to my list,
   176‚Üí**So that** I can track a task I need to do.
   177‚Üí
   178‚Üí### Acceptance Criteria
   179‚Üí
   180‚Üí- [ ] Input field for the todo title is prominently placed (above or at the top of the list)
   181‚Üí- [ ] Input has a visible `<label>` (e.g., "New todo") and helpful placeholder text
   182‚Üí  (e.g., "What do you need to do?")
   183‚Üí- [ ] Submitting the form adds the item to the list immediately
   184‚Üí- [ ] New items are added in the `Pending` state
   185‚Üí- [ ] After adding, the input field is cleared and focus returns to it (ready for the
   186‚Üí  next item)
   187‚Üí- [ ] Empty or whitespace-only submissions are silently ignored (no error message --
   188‚Üí  an accidental Enter press is not an intentional submission)
   189‚Üí- [ ] Title has a maximum length of 300 characters (agreed with domain architect)
   190‚Üí- [ ] Titles exceeding max length show a clear inline validation error
   191‚Üí- [ ] Form works without JavaScript (full page reload, item appears in list)
   192‚Üí- [ ] HTMX enhances: item is appended to the list without page reload
   193‚Üí- [ ] New item creation is logged with structured tracing
   194‚Üí- [ ] `aria-live` region announces "Todo added" (or similar) to screen readers after
   195‚Üí  HTMX swap
   196‚Üí
   197‚Üí### Edge Cases
   198‚Üí
   199‚Üí- Adding an item with only whitespace (silently ignored)
   200‚Üí- Pressing Enter with empty input (silently ignored)
   201‚Üí- Adding an item with very long text (at or beyond max length -- show error)
   202‚Üí- Adding an item with special characters, HTML, or emoji
   203‚Üí- Rapid successive additions (no duplicate submissions)
   204‚Üí- Adding when disconnected (graceful failure)
   205‚Üí
   206‚Üí### Success Metric
   207‚Üí
   208‚Üí- Adding a todo takes fewer than 5 seconds from thought to captured item. The flow
   209‚Üí  feels as fast as jotting something on a sticky note.
   210‚Üí
   211‚Üí---
   212‚Üí
   213‚Üí## US-6: Complete a Todo Item (Must Have)
   214‚Üí
   215‚Üí**As a** logged-in user,
   216‚Üí**I want to** mark a todo item as complete,
   217‚Üí**So that** I can see what I've accomplished and focus on what's left.
   218‚Üí
   219‚Üí### Acceptance Criteria
   220‚Üí
   221‚Üí- [ ] Each pending todo item has a mechanism to mark it complete (e.g., a checkbox or button)
   222‚Üí- [ ] The mechanism is keyboard accessible
   223‚Üí- [ ] Completing an item visually updates it immediately (e.g., strikethrough, dimmed,
   224‚Üí  moved to a "completed" section)
   225‚Üí- [ ] Completed items remain visible in the list (not hidden)
   226‚Üí- [ ] A visual indicator beyond color alone marks completed items (e.g., strikethrough + check)
   227‚Üí- [ ] Completion can be undone (mark as pending again) -- toggling state
   228‚Üí- [ ] State change works without JavaScript (form submission)
   229‚Üí- [ ] HTMX enhances: in-place update without page reload
   230‚Üí- [ ] State change is announced to screen readers via `aria-live` region
   231‚Üí- [ ] Completion event is logged with structured tracing
   232‚Üí- [ ] Focus remains on or near the toggled item after the update
   233‚Üí
   234‚Üí### Edge Cases
   235‚Üí
   236‚Üí- Completing an already-completed item (toggle back to pending)
   237‚Üí- Completing an item that was deleted by another tab (graceful error)
   238‚Üí- Rapid toggling (no race conditions)
   239‚Üí
   240‚Üí### Success Metric
   241‚Üí
   242‚Üí- Completing a todo is a single action (one click or keypress). The visual feedback
   243‚Üí  is immediate and satisfying.
   244‚Üí
   245‚Üí---
   246‚Üí
   247‚Üí## US-7: Delete a Todo Item (Must Have)
   248‚Üí
   249‚Üí**As a** logged-in user,
   250‚Üí**I want to** delete a todo item I no longer need,
   251‚Üí**So that** my list stays clean and focused.
   252‚Üí
   253‚Üí### Acceptance Criteria
   254‚Üí
   255‚Üí- [ ] Each todo item has a delete action (button, not a link -- this is a destructive action)
   256‚Üí- [ ] Delete button has an accessible label (e.g., "Delete: Buy groceries")
   257‚Üí- [ ] Deletion removes the item from the list immediately
   258‚Üí- [ ] Deletion is permanent (no undo in MVP -- but confirm before deleting)
   259‚Üí- [ ] Confirmation mechanism before deletion (e.g., "Are you sure?" or visually distinct
   260‚Üí  confirm/cancel) -- keep it lightweight, not a modal dialog
   261‚Üí- [ ] After deletion, focus moves to the next item in the list (or to the "add" input
   262‚Üí  if the list is now empty)
   263‚Üí- [ ] Deletion works without JavaScript (form submission, page reload)
   264‚Üí- [ ] HTMX enhances: item is removed from DOM without page reload
   265‚Üí- [ ] Screen reader announcement: "Todo deleted" via `aria-live` region
   266‚Üí- [ ] Deletion event is logged with structured tracing
   267‚Üí- [ ] Deleting someone else's todo is not possible (authorization)
   268‚Üí
   269‚Üí### Edge Cases
   270‚Üí
   271‚Üí- Deleting the last item in the list (transitions to empty state)
   272‚Üí- Deleting an item that was already deleted (e.g., in another tab)
   273‚Üí- Confirmation dismissed (item should not be deleted)
   274‚Üí
   275‚Üí### Success Metric
   276‚Üí
   277‚Üí- Deleting a todo is quick but safe. Users never accidentally lose a todo item.
   278‚Üí
   279‚Üí---
   280‚Üí
   281‚Üí## US-8: Edit a Todo Item Title (Should Have)
   282‚Üí
   283‚Üí**As a** logged-in user,
   284‚Üí**I want to** edit the title of an existing todo item,
   285‚Üí**So that** I can fix typos or update the description without deleting and re-creating.
   286‚Üí
   287‚Üí### Acceptance Criteria
   288‚Üí
   289‚Üí- [ ] Each todo item has a visible edit button (pencil icon) as the primary affordance
   290‚Üí  (click-on-title as an optional progressive enhancement for power users, but the icon
   291‚Üí  is what new users will discover)
   292‚Üí- [ ] Edit mode replaces the title with an input field pre-filled with the current title
   293‚Üí- [ ] Input has a visible `<label>` (may be visually hidden if the context is clear,
   294‚Üí  but must be present for screen readers)
   295‚Üí- [ ] Save and cancel actions are available (e.g., Enter to save, Escape to cancel, or
   296‚Üí  explicit Save/Cancel buttons)
   297‚Üí- [ ] Same validation as adding: non-empty, max length, no whitespace-only
   298‚Üí- [ ] Validation errors shown inline
   299‚Üí- [ ] After saving, the item returns to display mode with the updated title
   300‚Üí- [ ] After canceling, the original title is restored
   301‚Üí- [ ] Edit works without JavaScript (navigate to an edit page/form, save redirects back)
   302‚Üí- [ ] HTMX enhances: inline editing without page navigation
   303‚Üí- [ ] Focus management: on entering edit mode, focus moves to the input; on saving/canceling,
   304‚Üí  focus returns to the item
   305‚Üí- [ ] Screen reader: state change announced via `aria-live` region
   306‚Üí- [ ] Edit event is logged with structured tracing
   307‚Üí
   308‚Üí### Edge Cases
   309‚Üí
   310‚Üí- Editing to an empty title (validation error)
   311‚Üí- Editing while another tab has deleted the item
   312‚Üí- Editing a completed item (should work the same way)
   313‚Üí- Very long replacement text
   314‚Üí- Pressing Escape after making changes (discard changes)
   315‚Üí- Saving with no changes (no-op, no error)
   316‚Üí
   317‚Üí### Success Metric
   318‚Üí
   319‚Üí- Fixing a typo in a todo item takes under 5 seconds. The inline editing feels natural
   320‚Üí  and doesn't interrupt the user's flow.

   353‚Üí## Part 2: V1 GTD (Getting Things Done)
   354‚Üí
   355‚ÜíThe product is evolving from a simple todo list into a full GTD system. These stories
   356‚Üídefine the V1 GTD scope. See `docs/gtd-product-discovery.md` for the complete product
   357‚Üídiscovery document.
   358‚Üí
   359‚Üí### US-GTD-1: Capture to Inbox
   360‚Üí
   361‚Üí**As a** GTD practitioner,
   362‚Üí**I want to** quickly capture anything on my mind into my inbox,
   363‚Üí**So that** I can get it out of my head and process it later.
   364‚Üí
   365‚Üí#### Acceptance Criteria
   366‚Üí
   367‚Üí- [ ] Inbox capture input is always accessible (prominent on the main page, and available
   368‚Üí  via a persistent capture input on every authenticated page)
   369‚Üí- [ ] Capture is fast: type and press Enter, item appears in inbox
   370‚Üí- [ ] No categorization required at capture time (that happens during clarify)
   371‚Üí- [ ] Inbox items show in a list ordered by capture time (newest first)
   372‚Üí- [ ] Inbox count is visible in the navigation so users know how many items need processing
   373‚Üí- [ ] Input has a visible `<label>` and helpful placeholder text
   374‚Üí- [ ] After capturing, the input field is cleared and focus returns to it
   375‚Üí- [ ] Empty or whitespace-only submissions are silently ignored
   376‚Üí- [ ] Title has a maximum length of 300 characters
   377‚Üí- [ ] Works without JavaScript (full page reload); HTMX enhances
   378‚Üí- [ ] `aria-live` region announces "Captured to inbox" after HTMX swap
   379‚Üí- [ ] Capture event is logged with structured tracing
   380‚Üí
   381‚Üí#### Edge Cases
   382‚Üí- Adding with only whitespace (silently ignored)
   383‚Üí- Rapid successive captures
   384‚Üí- Very long text (at or beyond max length)
   385‚Üí- Special characters, HTML, emoji
   386‚Üí
   387‚Üí#### Success Metric
   388‚ÜíCapturing a thought takes under 3 seconds from thought to inbox.
   389‚Üí
   390‚Üí---
   391‚Üí
   392‚Üí### US-GTD-2: Clarify Inbox Items
   393‚Üí
   394‚Üí**As a** GTD practitioner,
   395‚Üí**I want to** process each inbox item through the GTD clarify workflow,
   396‚Üí**So that** I can decide what each item means and where it belongs.
   397‚Üí
   398‚Üí#### Acceptance Criteria
   399‚Üí
   400‚Üí- [ ] User can process inbox items one at a time (process view for a single item)
   401‚Üí- [ ] For each item, the user sees the original capture text and can choose:
   402‚Üí  - **Next Action**: Specify action text (may keep or edit the captured text), select
   403‚Üí    a context, optionally link to an existing or new project
   404‚Üí  - **Project**: Create a new project with title and a first next action
   405‚Üí  - **Waiting For**: Move to waiting for list with who/what information
   406‚Üí  - **Someday/Maybe**: Park as a future idea
   407‚Üí  - **Trash**: Delete permanently
   408‚Üí- [ ] After clarifying, item leaves the inbox and appears in its destination list
   409‚Üí- [ ] Processing a single item to Next Action requires a context selection
   410‚Üí- [ ] User can skip an item and come back to it later
   411‚Üí- [ ] Clarify page shows the current inbox count (items remaining)
   412‚Üí- [ ] All form inputs have visible labels
   413‚Üí- [ ] Works without JavaScript; HTMX enhances
   414‚Üí- [ ] Screen reader announces the result of clarification via `aria-live` region
   415‚Üí
   416‚Üí#### Edge Cases
   417‚Üí- Last inbox item processed (show success message, link back to dashboard)
   418‚Üí- Clarifying to a context that was just created
   419‚Üí- Editing the title during clarification
   420‚Üí- Canceling mid-clarification (item stays in inbox)
   421‚Üí
   422‚Üí#### Success Metric
   423‚ÜíProcessing a single inbox item takes under 15 seconds.
   424‚Üí
   425‚Üí---
   426‚Üí
   427‚Üí### US-GTD-3: View and Work from Next Actions
   428‚Üí
   429‚Üí**As a** GTD practitioner,
   430‚Üí**I want to** see my next actions filtered by context,
   431‚Üí**So that** I can quickly find what I can do right now based on where I am.
   432‚Üí
   433‚Üí#### Acceptance Criteria
   434‚Üí
   435‚Üí- [ ] Next Actions page shows all active (not completed) next actions
   436‚Üí- [ ] Actions can be filtered by context (e.g., show only @computer actions)
   437‚Üí- [ ] Default view shows all contexts with context headings as groupings
   438‚Üí- [ ] Each action shows its title, context, and associated project name (if any)
   439‚Üí- [ ] User can mark an action as complete (removes from active list)
   440‚Üí- [ ] User can delete an action
   441‚Üí- [ ] User can edit an action's title
   442‚Üí- [ ] Context filter is a set of links/buttons, not a dropdown (scannable)
   443‚Üí- [ ] Page has clear heading hierarchy
   444‚Üí- [ ] Works without JavaScript; HTMX enhances for in-place updates
   445‚Üí- [ ] Screen reader announces state changes via `aria-live` region
   446‚Üí
   447‚Üí#### Edge Cases
   448‚Üí- No actions in a given context (show empty state per context)
   449‚Üí- No actions at all (show helpful empty state)
   450‚Üí- Filtering to a context with zero actions
   451‚Üí- Action linked to a completed/dropped project
   452‚Üí
   453‚Üí#### Success Metric
   454‚ÜíUser can find and start working on a relevant action within 10 seconds.
   455‚Üí
   456‚Üí---
   457‚Üí
   458‚Üí### US-GTD-4: Manage Projects
   459‚Üí
   460‚Üí**As a** GTD practitioner,
   461‚Üí**I want to** see all my active projects and their next actions,
   462‚Üí**So that** I can ensure every project is moving forward.
   463‚Üí
   464‚Üí#### Acceptance Criteria
   465‚Üí
   466‚Üí- [ ] Projects list shows all active projects
   467‚Üí- [ ] Each project shows its title and the count of active next actions
   468‚Üí- [ ] Stalled projects (zero next actions) are visually flagged
   469‚Üí- [ ] User can view a project detail page showing all linked next actions (active and
   470‚Üí  completed) and waiting-for items
   471‚Üí- [ ] User can add a project directly (without going through inbox)
   472‚Üí- [ ] User can add a next action directly to a project (with context selection)
   473‚Üí- [ ] User can complete a project (marks as finished)
   474‚Üí- [ ] User can drop a project (abandoned, different from completed)
   475‚Üí- [ ] User can edit a project's title
   476‚Üí- [ ] User can delete a project (with confirmation; warns about linked actions)
   477‚Üí- [ ] Works without JavaScript; HTMX enhances
   478‚Üí
   479‚Üí#### Edge Cases
   480‚Üí- Project with no next actions (stalled -- flagged during weekly review)
   481‚Üí- Deleting a project with active next actions (what happens to them?)
   482‚Üí- Completing a project with remaining active next actions
   483‚Üí- Very long project title
   484‚Üí
   485‚Üí#### Success Metric
   486‚ÜíDuring weekly review, user can verify all projects have next actions in under 2 minutes.
   487‚Üí
   488‚Üí---
   489‚Üí
   490‚Üí### US-GTD-5: Waiting For List
   491‚Üí
   492‚Üí**As a** GTD practitioner,
   493‚Üí**I want to** track items I'm waiting on from others,
   494‚Üí**So that** I can follow up at the right time and nothing falls through the cracks.
   495‚Üí
   496‚Üí#### Acceptance Criteria
   497‚Üí
   498‚Üí- [ ] Waiting For list shows all items the user is waiting on
   499‚Üí- [ ] Each item shows: title, who/what it's waiting on, date added
   500‚Üí- [ ] Items are sorted by date added (oldest first -- longest-waiting items at top)
   501‚Üí- [ ] User can add items directly to Waiting For (with title and waiting-on text)
   502‚Üí- [ ] User can resolve a Waiting For item (removes from list)
   503‚Üí- [ ] User can convert a Waiting For item to a Next Action (with context selection)
   504‚Üí- [ ] User can edit a Waiting For item's title and waiting-on text
   505‚Üí- [ ] User can delete a Waiting For item
   506‚Üí- [ ] User can optionally link a Waiting For item to a project
   507‚Üí- [ ] Works without JavaScript; HTMX enhances
   508‚Üí
   509‚Üí#### Edge Cases
   510‚Üí- Empty Waiting For list (show helpful message)
   511‚Üí- Very long waiting-on description
   512‚Üí- Resolving the last Waiting For item linked to a project
   513‚Üí
   514‚Üí#### Success Metric
   515‚ÜíUser can see everything they are waiting on in one glance.
   516‚Üí
   517‚Üí---
   518‚Üí
   519‚Üí### US-GTD-6: Someday/Maybe List
   520‚Üí
   521‚Üí**As a** GTD practitioner,
   522‚Üí**I want to** park ideas and future possibilities,
   523‚Üí**So that** they don't clutter my action lists but aren't forgotten either.
   524‚Üí
   525‚Üí#### Acceptance Criteria
   526‚Üí
   527‚Üí- [ ] Someday/Maybe list shows all parked ideas
   528‚Üí- [ ] User can add items directly to Someday/Maybe
   529‚Üí- [ ] User can activate an item (moves to inbox for re-clarification)
   530‚Üí- [ ] User can delete items that are no longer interesting
   531‚Üí- [ ] User can edit an item's title
   532‚Üí- [ ] List is reviewed during the weekly review (Get Creative phase)
   533‚Üí- [ ] Works without JavaScript; HTMX enhances
   534‚Üí
   535‚Üí#### Edge Cases
   536‚Üí- Empty Someday/Maybe list
   537‚Üí- Activating an item (goes to inbox, not directly to Next Actions)
   538‚Üí
   539‚Üí#### Success Metric
   540‚ÜíIdeas have a home that is not the user's head or their active lists.
   541‚Üí
   542‚Üí---
   543‚Üí
   544‚Üí### US-GTD-7: Weekly Review
   545‚Üí
   546‚Üí**As a** GTD practitioner,
   547‚Üí**I want to** be guided through a weekly review process,
   548‚Üí**So that** I can keep my system current and trustworthy.
   549‚Üí
   550‚Üí#### Acceptance Criteria
   551‚Üí
   552‚Üí- [ ] Weekly Review is accessible from the main navigation
   553‚Üí- [ ] Review follows three phases:
   554‚Üí  1. **Get Clear**: Shows inbox count, prompts user to process inbox to zero. Links to
   555‚Üí     inbox processing. Phase is complete when inbox is empty.
   556‚Üí  2. **Get Current**: Shows each active project; user confirms next action exists or adds
   557‚Üí     one. Shows Waiting For items; user confirms still waiting or resolves. Shows Next
   558‚Üí     Actions; user confirms still relevant or removes/defers.
   559‚Üí  3. **Get Creative**: Shows Someday/Maybe list for review. Prompts for new captures.
   560‚Üí- [ ] Each phase has a clear completion state
   561‚Üí- [ ] User can navigate between phases freely (not forced linear)
   562‚Üí- [ ] Review progress is visible (which phases are done this session)
   563‚Üí- [ ] Phase completion is announced to screen readers
   564‚Üí- [ ] Works without JavaScript; HTMX enhances
   565‚Üí
   566‚Üí#### Edge Cases
   567‚Üí- Starting a review with empty inbox (phase 1 auto-completes)
   568‚Üí- No active projects (phase 2 project section is empty)
   569‚Üí- Empty Someday/Maybe list (phase 3 shows capture prompt only)
   570‚Üí
   571‚Üí#### Success Metric
   572‚ÜíA weekly review can be completed in under 30 minutes.
   573‚Üí
   574‚Üí---
   575‚Üí
   576‚Üí### US-GTD-8: Navigation and Dashboard
   577‚Üí
   578‚Üí**As a** GTD practitioner,
   579‚Üí**I want to** navigate between all GTD lists and see a summary of my system's state,
   580‚Üí**So that** I can access any part of my GTD system quickly and know what needs attention.
   581‚Üí
   582‚Üí#### Acceptance Criteria
   583‚Üí
   584‚Üí- [ ] Main navigation includes: Inbox (with count), Next Actions, Projects, Waiting For,
   585‚Üí  Someday/Maybe, Weekly Review
   586‚Üí- [ ] Dashboard/home page shows a summary:
   587‚Üí  - Inbox count (items needing processing)
   588‚Üí  - Stalled projects count (projects without next actions)
   589‚Üí  - Total active next actions count
   590‚Üí  - Waiting For count
   591‚Üí- [ ] Navigation indicates the current page (aria-current="page")
   592‚Üí- [ ] Navigation is accessible: keyboard operable, screen reader landmarks
   593‚Üí- [ ] Logout remains accessible from every page
   594‚Üí- [ ] Quick capture input is available on every authenticated page
   595‚Üí- [ ] Works without JavaScript
   596‚Üí
   597‚Üí#### Edge Cases
   598‚Üí- All counts are zero (fresh user -- show getting started guidance)
   599‚Üí- Very high counts (display doesn't break)
   600‚Üí
   601‚Üí#### Success Metric
   602‚ÜíAny list is reachable in one click from any page.
   603‚Üí
   604‚Üí---
   605‚Üí
   606‚Üí## V1 GTD Priority and Implementation Order
   607‚Üí
   608‚ÜíFollowing the walking skeleton principle:
   609‚Üí
   610‚Üí1. **Navigation + Dashboard** -- Multi-page structure, nav bar, route skeleton
   611‚Üí2. **Inbox Capture** (US-GTD-1) -- New entry point
   612‚Üí3. **Contexts** -- Domain type + DB + default contexts for new users
   613‚Üí4. **Next Actions with Contexts** (US-GTD-3) -- Core working list
   614‚Üí5. **Clarify Workflow** (US-GTD-2) -- Processing inbox items
   615‚Üí6. **Projects** (US-GTD-4) -- Multi-step outcome tracking
   616‚Üí7. **Waiting For** (US-GTD-5) -- Delegation tracking
   617‚Üí8. **Someday/Maybe** (US-GTD-6) -- Idea parking
   618‚Üí9. **Weekly Review** (US-GTD-7) -- The review process
   619‚Üí10. **Data Migration + Cleanup** -- Migrate old todos, remove legacy code
   620‚Üí
   621‚ÜíEach step follows the Atomic Green Step pipeline. Each step delivers a usable increment.
   622‚Üí
   623‚Üí---
   624‚Üí
   625‚Üí## Product Success Criteria (V1 GTD)
   626‚Üí
   627‚ÜíV1 GTD is successful when:
   628‚Üí
   629‚Üí1. A user can capture items to their inbox and process them to zero
   630‚Üí2. Clarified items land in the correct list (Next Actions, Projects, Waiting For,
   631‚Üí   Someday/Maybe, or trash)
   632‚Üí3. Next Actions are filterable by context
   633‚Üí4. Every active project has at least one next action (stalled projects are flagged)
   634‚Üí5. The Weekly Review guided flow keeps the system current
   635‚Üí6. The entire system is accessible (WCAG 2.2 AA)
   636‚Üí7. The system works without JavaScript (progressive enhancement)
   637‚Üí8. A first-time GTD user can understand the system without external instructions
   638‚Üí9. All existing auth functionality continues to work
   639‚Üí10. All tests pass (Rust integration + Playwright e2e + axe-core a11y)
   640‚Üí
   641‚ÜíWe will know this is working when a user can complete the full GTD loop (capture ->
   642‚Üíclarify -> organize -> review -> engage) without hesitation.

# User Stories

## Part 1: MVP (Completed)

These user stories define the MVP scope. They are conversations, not contracts -- the
whole team owns them and anyone can propose changes with good reasoning.

Priority order follows the walking skeleton approach: get the thinnest slice working
end-to-end first, then layer on features.

---

## US-1: User Registration (Must Have)

**As a** new user,
**I want to** create an account with my email and password,
**So that** I have a personal, private space to manage my todos.

### Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] User can navigate to a registration page from the login page
- [ ] Registration form has fields: email, password, password confirmation
- [ ] **Password progressive enhancement** (consensus: Marty, Steve Krug, Heydon):
  - **No-JS baseline**: Email + password + password confirmation. Confirm field catches
    typos. Both password fields use `autocomplete="new-password"`.
  - **JS enhancement**: Password visibility toggle replaces confirm field. Toggle is a
    `<button type="button">` with `aria-label` ("Show password" / "Hide password") and
    `aria-pressed`. When toggle is active, confirm field is removed from DOM (not just
    hidden). Input type changes between `password` and `text`.
  - This is the agreed approach -- no further discussion needed.
- [ ] Every form input has a visible `<label>` element
- [ ] Submit button label is "Create account" (action words, not "Submit" or "Register")
- [ ] Successful registration creates a new user account and redirects to the todo list
  (user is automatically logged in)
- [ ] Email must be a valid email format
- [ ] Password must be at least 8 characters
- [ ] Password and password confirmation must match (no-JS baseline); or password
  verified via visibility toggle (JS enhancement)
- [ ] If email is already registered, show a secure, helpful error message that does NOT
  reveal whether the email exists (account enumeration prevention): "Something went
  wrong. If you already have an account, try signing in instead." with "signing in" as
  a link to the login page
- [ ] Validation errors are shown inline next to the relevant field
- [ ] Validation errors are associated with inputs via `aria-describedby`
- [ ] Password is hashed with Argon2 before storage (never stored in plaintext)
- [ ] Registration event is logged with structured tracing (no PII in logs)
- [ ] Form works without JavaScript (standard HTML form submission)
- [ ] HTMX enhances with inline validation and no full-page reload

### Edge Cases

- Email with unusual but valid format (e.g., `user+tag@example.com`)
- Very long email or password (enforce reasonable max lengths)
- SQL injection attempts in email/password fields (parameterized queries prevent this)
- Concurrent registration with the same email
- Empty form submission
- Whitespace-only inputs
- Password exactly at minimum length

### Success Metric

- A user can register and immediately start using their todo list in under 30 seconds.

---

## US-2: User Login (Must Have)

**As a** registered user,
**I want to** log in with my email and password,
**So that** I can access my personal todo list.

### Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Login page is the default landing page for unauthenticated users
- [ ] Login form has fields: email, password
- [ ] Every form input has a visible `<label>` element
- [ ] Successful login redirects to the user's todo list
- [ ] Submit button label is "Sign in" (action words, not "Submit" or "Login")
- [ ] Invalid credentials show a friendly generic error: "That email or password didn't
  work. Try again." (do NOT reveal which field is wrong)
- [ ] Error message is announced to screen readers via `aria-live` region
- [ ] Login page has a link to the registration page
- [ ] "Forgot password?" link is present below the form. For MVP, it leads to a
  placeholder page explaining the feature is coming soon. (Password reset is out of
  MVP scope but users expect to see the link.)
- [ ] Session is created server-side on successful login
- [ ] Failed login attempts are logged with structured tracing (no PII)
- [ ] Form works without JavaScript
- [ ] HTMX enhances with no full-page reload

### Edge Cases

- User who has not registered tries to log in
- Correct email, wrong password
- Case sensitivity in email (emails should be case-insensitive)
- Brute force attempts (rate limiting is a v2 concern, but log failed attempts)
- Session fixation (generate new session ID on login)
- User tries to access todo list URL directly without being logged in (redirect to login)

### Success Metric

- A returning user can log in and see their todo list in under 10 seconds.

---

## US-3: User Logout (Must Have)

**As a** logged-in user,
**I want to** log out of my account,
**So that** my todo list is protected when I'm done using the app.

### Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Logout button/link is visible on every authenticated page
- [ ] Clicking logout destroys the server-side session
- [ ] After logout, user is redirected to the login page
- [ ] After logout, the back button does not show authenticated content (no cache leaks)
- [ ] Logout works without JavaScript (standard link or form submission)
- [ ] Logout event is logged with structured tracing

### Edge Cases

- Double-clicking logout
- Logout when session has already expired
- Logout from multiple tabs (session destroyed on server; other tabs redirect on next request)

### Success Metric

- User can log out with a single click and is confident their session is ended.

---

## US-4: View Todo List (Must Have)

**As a** logged-in user,
**I want to** see my todo list when I log in,
**So that** I know what I need to do.

### Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Todo list is the main screen after login
- [ ] Page has a clear heading (e.g., "My Todos") as the `<h1>`
- [ ] Todo items are displayed in a semantic list (`<ul>` or `<ol>`) so screen readers
  automatically announce the item count (e.g., "list, 5 items")
- [ ] Each item shows its title and completion status
- [ ] Completed items are visually distinct from pending items (not just color --
  a secondary indicator like strikethrough text)
- [ ] **Empty state**: When the user has no todos, show a brief, friendly message
  (e.g., "No todos yet.") -- not a blank screen. The add-todo input above makes the
  next action obvious; don't over-explain.
- [ ] Page title (`<title>`) is descriptive (e.g., "My Todos -- Todo List")
- [ ] The page includes proper landmark regions (`<main>`, `<nav>`, `<header>`)
- [ ] Skip link to main content is the first focusable element
- [ ] Each user can only see their own todos (authorization check)
- [ ] Page works without JavaScript

### Edge Cases

- User with zero todos (empty state)
- User with one todo
- User with many todos (100+) -- still performant and usable
- Todo titles with special characters, HTML entities, or very long text
- Accessing another user's todo list URL (must return 403 or redirect)

### Success Metric

- A user can see their entire todo list within 1 second of logging in. The list is
  scannable -- the user's eye goes directly to what needs doing.

---

## US-5: Add a Todo Item (Must Have)

**As a** logged-in user,
**I want to** add a new todo item to my list,
**So that** I can track a task I need to do.

### Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Input field for the todo title is prominently placed (above or at the top of the list)
- [ ] Input has a visible `<label>` (e.g., "New todo") and helpful placeholder text
  (e.g., "What do you need to do?")
- [ ] Submitting the form adds the item to the list immediately
- [ ] New items are added in the `Pending` state
- [ ] After adding, the input field is cleared and focus returns to it (ready for the
  next item)
- [ ] Empty or whitespace-only submissions are silently ignored (no error message --
  an accidental Enter press is not an intentional submission)
- [ ] Title has a maximum length of 300 characters (agreed with domain architect)
- [ ] Titles exceeding max length show a clear inline validation error
- [ ] Form works without JavaScript (full page reload, item appears in list)
- [ ] HTMX enhances: item is appended to the list without page reload
- [ ] New item creation is logged with structured tracing
- [ ] `aria-live` region announces "Todo added" (or similar) to screen readers after
  HTMX swap

### Edge Cases

- Adding an item with only whitespace (silently ignored)
- Pressing Enter with empty input (silently ignored)
- Adding an item with very long text (at or beyond max length -- show error)
- Adding an item with special characters, HTML, or emoji
- Rapid successive additions (no duplicate submissions)
- Adding when disconnected (graceful failure)

### Success Metric

- Adding a todo takes fewer than 5 seconds from thought to captured item. The flow
  feels as fast as jotting something on a sticky note.

---

## US-6: Complete a Todo Item (Must Have)

**As a** logged-in user,
**I want to** mark a todo item as complete,
**So that** I can see what I've accomplished and focus on what's left.

### Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Each pending todo item has a mechanism to mark it complete (e.g., a checkbox or button)
- [ ] The mechanism is keyboard accessible
- [ ] Completing an item visually updates it immediately (e.g., strikethrough, dimmed,
  moved to a "completed" section)
- [ ] Completed items remain visible in the list (not hidden)
- [ ] A visual indicator beyond color alone marks completed items (e.g., strikethrough + check)
- [ ] Completion can be undone (mark as pending again) -- toggling state
- [ ] State change works without JavaScript (form submission)
- [ ] HTMX enhances: in-place update without page reload
- [ ] State change is announced to screen readers via `aria-live` region
- [ ] Completion event is logged with structured tracing
- [ ] Focus remains on or near the toggled item after the update

### Edge Cases

- Completing an already-completed item (toggle back to pending)
- Completing an item that was deleted by another tab (graceful error)
- Rapid toggling (no race conditions)

### Success Metric

- Completing a todo is a single action (one click or keypress). The visual feedback
  is immediate and satisfying.

---

## US-7: Delete a Todo Item (Must Have)

**As a** logged-in user,
**I want to** delete a todo item I no longer need,
**So that** my list stays clean and focused.

### Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Each todo item has a delete action (button, not a link -- this is a destructive action)
- [ ] Delete button has an accessible label (e.g., "Delete: Buy groceries")
- [ ] Deletion removes the item from the list immediately
- [ ] Deletion is permanent (no undo in MVP -- but confirm before deleting)
- [ ] Confirmation mechanism before deletion (e.g., "Are you sure?" or visually distinct
  confirm/cancel) -- keep it lightweight, not a modal dialog
- [ ] After deletion, focus moves to the next item in the list (or to the "add" input
  if the list is now empty)
- [ ] Deletion works without JavaScript (form submission, page reload)
- [ ] HTMX enhances: item is removed from DOM without page reload
- [ ] Screen reader announcement: "Todo deleted" via `aria-live` region
- [ ] Deletion event is logged with structured tracing
- [ ] Deleting someone else's todo is not possible (authorization)

### Edge Cases

- Deleting the last item in the list (transitions to empty state)
- Deleting an item that was already deleted (e.g., in another tab)
- Confirmation dismissed (item should not be deleted)

### Success Metric

- Deleting a todo is quick but safe. Users never accidentally lose a todo item.

---

## US-8: Edit a Todo Item Title (Should Have)

**As a** logged-in user,
**I want to** edit the title of an existing todo item,
**So that** I can fix typos or update the description without deleting and re-creating.

### Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Each todo item has a visible edit button (pencil icon) as the primary affordance
  (click-on-title as an optional progressive enhancement for power users, but the icon
  is what new users will discover)
- [ ] Edit mode replaces the title with an input field pre-filled with the current title
- [ ] Input has a visible `<label>` (may be visually hidden if the context is clear,
  but must be present for screen readers)
- [ ] Save and cancel actions are available (e.g., Enter to save, Escape to cancel, or
  explicit Save/Cancel buttons)
- [ ] Same validation as adding: non-empty, max length, no whitespace-only
- [ ] Validation errors shown inline
- [ ] After saving, the item returns to display mode with the updated title
- [ ] After canceling, the original title is restored
- [ ] Edit works without JavaScript (navigate to an edit page/form, save redirects back)
- [ ] HTMX enhances: inline editing without page navigation
- [ ] Focus management: on entering edit mode, focus moves to the input; on saving/canceling,
  focus returns to the item
- [ ] Screen reader: state change announced via `aria-live` region
- [ ] Edit event is logged with structured tracing

### Edge Cases

- Editing to an empty title (validation error)
- Editing while another tab has deleted the item
- Editing a completed item (should work the same way)
- Very long replacement text
- Pressing Escape after making changes (discard changes)
- Saving with no changes (no-op, no error)

### Success Metric

- Fixing a typo in a todo item takes under 5 seconds. The inline editing feels natural
  and doesn't interrupt the user's flow.

---

## MVP Implementation Order (Completed)

All MVP features have been implemented and shipped:

1. Project scaffolding (Task #1)
2. Walking skeleton (Task #2)
3. Design tokens (Task #3)
4. US-1: Registration (Task #4-5)
5. US-2: Login + US-3: Logout (Task #5-6)
6. US-4: View todo list + US-5: Add todo (Task #7)
7. US-6: Complete todo (Task #8)
8. US-7: Delete todo (Task #9)
9. US-8: Edit todo title (Task #20)

---

## Product Success Criteria (MVP -- Achieved)

The MVP was successful:
- 97 Rust integration tests + 6 Playwright e2e tests all passing
- WCAG 2.2 AA accessible (axe-core verified)
- Works without JavaScript (progressive enhancement with HTMX)
- Full TDD coverage
- CI green

---

---

## Part 2: V1 GTD (Getting Things Done)

The product is evolving from a simple todo list into a full GTD system. These stories
define the V1 GTD scope. See `docs/gtd-product-discovery.md` for the complete product
discovery document.

### US-GTD-1: Capture to Inbox

**As a** GTD practitioner,
**I want to** quickly capture anything on my mind into my inbox,
**So that** I can get it out of my head and process it later.

#### Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Inbox capture input is always accessible (prominent on the main page, and available
  via a persistent capture input on every authenticated page)
- [ ] Capture is fast: type and press Enter, item appears in inbox
- [ ] No categorization required at capture time (that happens during clarify)
- [ ] Inbox items show in a list ordered by capture time (newest first)
- [ ] Inbox count is visible in the navigation so users know how many items need processing
- [ ] Input has a visible `<label>` and helpful placeholder text
- [ ] After capturing, the input field is cleared and focus returns to it
- [ ] Empty or whitespace-only submissions are silently ignored
- [ ] Title has a maximum length of 300 characters
- [ ] Works without JavaScript (full page reload); HTMX enhances
- [ ] `aria-live` region announces "Captured to inbox" after HTMX swap
- [ ] Capture event is logged with structured tracing

#### Edge Cases
- Adding with only whitespace (silently ignored)
- Rapid successive captures
- Very long text (at or beyond max length)
- Special characters, HTML, emoji

#### Success Metric
Capturing a thought takes under 3 seconds from thought to inbox.

---

### US-GTD-2: Clarify Inbox Items

**As a** GTD practitioner,
**I want to** process each inbox item through the GTD clarify workflow,
**So that** I can decide what each item means and where it belongs.

#### Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] User can process inbox items one at a time (process view for a single item)
- [ ] For each item, the user sees the original capture text and can choose:
  - **Next Action**: Specify action text (may keep or edit the captured text), select
    a context, optionally link to an existing or new project
  - **Project**: Create a new project with title and a first next action
  - **Waiting For**: Move to waiting for list with who/what information
  - **Someday/Maybe**: Park as a future idea
  - **Trash**: Delete permanently
- [ ] After clarifying, item leaves the inbox and appears in its destination list
- [ ] Processing a single item to Next Action requires a context selection
- [ ] User can skip an item and come back to it later
- [ ] Clarify page shows the current inbox count (items remaining)
- [ ] All form inputs have visible labels
- [ ] Works without JavaScript; HTMX enhances
- [ ] Screen reader announces the result of clarification via `aria-live` region

#### Edge Cases
- Last inbox item processed (show success message, link back to dashboard)
- Clarifying to a context that was just created
- Editing the title during clarification
- Canceling mid-clarification (item stays in inbox)

#### Success Metric
Processing a single inbox item takes under 15 seconds.

---

### US-GTD-3: View and Work from Next Actions

**As a** GTD practitioner,
**I want to** see my next actions filtered by context,
**So that** I can quickly find what I can do right now based on where I am.

#### Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Next Actions page shows all active (not completed) next actions
- [ ] Actions can be filtered by context (e.g., show only @computer actions)
- [ ] Default view shows all contexts with context headings as groupings
- [ ] Each action shows its title, context, and associated project name (if any)
- [ ] User can mark an action as complete (removes from active list)
- [ ] User can delete an action
- [ ] User can edit an action's title
- [ ] Context filter is a set of links/buttons, not a dropdown (scannable)
- [ ] Page has clear heading hierarchy
- [ ] Works without JavaScript; HTMX enhances for in-place updates
- [ ] Screen reader announces state changes via `aria-live` region

#### Edge Cases
- No actions in a given context (show empty state per context)
- No actions at all (show helpful empty state)
- Filtering to a context with zero actions
- Action linked to a completed/dropped project

#### Success Metric
User can find and start working on a relevant action within 10 seconds.

---

### US-GTD-4: Manage Projects

**As a** GTD practitioner,
**I want to** see all my active projects and their next actions,
**So that** I can ensure every project is moving forward.

#### Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Projects list shows all active projects
- [ ] Each project shows its title and the count of active next actions
- [ ] Stalled projects (zero next actions) are visually flagged
- [ ] User can view a project detail page showing all linked next actions (active and
  completed) and waiting-for items
- [ ] User can add a project directly (without going through inbox)
- [ ] User can add a next action directly to a project (with context selection)
- [ ] User can complete a project (marks as finished)
- [ ] User can drop a project (abandoned, different from completed)
- [ ] User can edit a project's title
- [ ] User can delete a project (with confirmation; warns about linked actions)
- [ ] Works without JavaScript; HTMX enhances

#### Edge Cases
- Project with no next actions (stalled -- flagged during weekly review)
- Deleting a project with active next actions (what happens to them?)
- Completing a project with remaining active next actions
- Very long project title

#### Success Metric
During weekly review, user can verify all projects have next actions in under 2 minutes.

---

### US-GTD-5: Waiting For List

**As a** GTD practitioner,
**I want to** track items I'm waiting on from others,
**So that** I can follow up at the right time and nothing falls through the cracks.

#### Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Waiting For list shows all items the user is waiting on
- [ ] Each item shows: title, who/what it's waiting on, date added
- [ ] Items are sorted by date added (oldest first -- longest-waiting items at top)
- [ ] User can add items directly to Waiting For (with title and waiting-on text)
- [ ] User can resolve a Waiting For item (removes from list)
- [ ] User can convert a Waiting For item to a Next Action (with context selection)
- [ ] User can edit a Waiting For item's title and waiting-on text
- [ ] User can delete a Waiting For item
- [ ] User can optionally link a Waiting For item to a project
- [ ] Works without JavaScript; HTMX enhances

#### Edge Cases
- Empty Waiting For list (show helpful message)
- Very long waiting-on description
- Resolving the last Waiting For item linked to a project

#### Success Metric
User can see everything they are waiting on in one glance.

---

### US-GTD-6: Someday/Maybe List

**As a** GTD practitioner,
**I want to** park ideas and future possibilities,
**So that** they don't clutter my action lists but aren't forgotten either.

#### Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Someday/Maybe list shows all parked ideas
- [ ] User can add items directly to Someday/Maybe
- [ ] User can activate an item (moves to inbox for re-clarification)
- [ ] User can delete items that are no longer interesting
- [ ] User can edit an item's title
- [ ] List is reviewed during the weekly review (Get Creative phase)
- [ ] Works without JavaScript; HTMX enhances

#### Edge Cases
- Empty Someday/Maybe list
- Activating an item (goes to inbox, not directly to Next Actions)

#### Success Metric
Ideas have a home that is not the user's head or their active lists.

---

### US-GTD-7: Weekly Review

**As a** GTD practitioner,
**I want to** be guided through a weekly review process,
**So that** I can keep my system current and trustworthy.

#### Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Weekly Review is accessible from the main navigation
- [ ] Review follows three phases:
  1. **Get Clear**: Shows inbox count, prompts user to process inbox to zero. Links to
     inbox processing. Phase is complete when inbox is empty.
  2. **Get Current**: Shows each active project; user confirms next action exists or adds
     one. Shows Waiting For items; user confirms still waiting or resolves. Shows Next
     Actions; user confirms still relevant or removes/defers.
  3. **Get Creative**: Shows Someday/Maybe list for review. Prompts for new captures.
- [ ] Each phase has a clear completion state
- [ ] User can navigate between phases freely (not forced linear)
- [ ] Review progress is visible (which phases are done this session)
- [ ] Phase completion is announced to screen readers
- [ ] Works without JavaScript; HTMX enhances

#### Edge Cases
- Starting a review with empty inbox (phase 1 auto-completes)
- No active projects (phase 2 project section is empty)
- Empty Someday/Maybe list (phase 3 shows capture prompt only)

#### Success Metric
A weekly review can be completed in under 30 minutes.

---

### US-GTD-8: Navigation and Dashboard

**As a** GTD practitioner,
**I want to** navigate between all GTD lists and see a summary of my system's state,
**So that** I can access any part of my GTD system quickly and know what needs attention.

#### Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Main navigation includes: Inbox (with count), Next Actions, Projects, Waiting For,
  Someday/Maybe, Weekly Review
- [ ] Dashboard/home page shows a summary:
  - Inbox count (items needing processing)
  - Stalled projects count (projects without next actions)
  - Total active next actions count
  - Waiting For count
- [ ] Navigation indicates the current page (aria-current="page")
- [ ] Navigation is accessible: keyboard operable, screen reader landmarks
- [ ] Logout remains accessible from every page
- [ ] Quick capture input is available on every authenticated page
- [ ] Works without JavaScript

#### Edge Cases
- All counts are zero (fresh user -- show getting started guidance)
- Very high counts (display doesn't break)

#### Success Metric
Any list is reachable in one click from any page.

---

## V1 GTD Priority and Implementation Order

Following the walking skeleton principle:

1. **Navigation + Dashboard** -- Multi-page structure, nav bar, route skeleton
2. **Inbox Capture** (US-GTD-1) -- New entry point
3. **Contexts** -- Domain type + DB + default contexts for new users
4. **Next Actions with Contexts** (US-GTD-3) -- Core working list
5. **Clarify Workflow** (US-GTD-2) -- Processing inbox items
6. **Projects** (US-GTD-4) -- Multi-step outcome tracking
7. **Waiting For** (US-GTD-5) -- Delegation tracking
8. **Someday/Maybe** (US-GTD-6) -- Idea parking
9. **Weekly Review** (US-GTD-7) -- The review process
10. **Data Migration + Cleanup** -- Migrate old todos, remove legacy code

Each step follows the Atomic Green Step pipeline. Each step delivers a usable increment.

---

## Product Success Criteria (V1 GTD)

V1 GTD is successful when:

1. A user can capture items to their inbox and process them to zero
2. Clarified items land in the correct list (Next Actions, Projects, Waiting For,
   Someday/Maybe, or trash)
3. Next Actions are filterable by context
4. Every active project has at least one next action (stalled projects are flagged)
5. The Weekly Review guided flow keeps the system current
6. The entire system is accessible (WCAG 2.2 AA)
7. The system works without JavaScript (progressive enhancement)
8. A first-time GTD user can understand the system without external instructions
9. All existing auth functionality continues to work

input.content:
Marty, excellent product discovery. These documents are thorough and well-structured. Here's my feedback from the frontend/CSS perspective, including answers to your open questions.

## Overall Feedback

The scope is right-sized. The 8 user stories cover the full GTD loop without overreaching. The implementation order (nav first, then inbox, then next actions, then clarify) makes sense from a frontend standpoint -- each step builds on the UI infrastructure of the previous one.

## Answers to Open Questions

### 1. Default Contexts
Your suggestion of @computer, @home, @errands, @phone, @office, @anywhere is good. From a CSS/UI perspective, I'd note:
- **Keep the list short** (5-6 is perfect). More than 6 defaults creates visual clutter in the context filter nav and the clarify form's context `<select>`.
- **@anywhere is important** -- it's the catch-all that means "no context required." Without it, users feel forced to pick a context for things that don't have one.
- I'd drop **@office** for v1 if we want to keep it to 5. It overlaps heavily with @computer for knowledge workers. Users who need it can add it as a custom context.
- Suggested default set: **@computer, @home, @errands, @phone, @anywhere** (5 defaults)

### 2. Where Do Completed Actions Go?
I agree with your recommendation **(a) -- removed from Next Actions, visible in project view**. From a CSS standpoint:
- The Next Actions view stays clean -- only actionable items, no clutter
- Project detail pages show both active and completed actions, giving a sense of progress
- We need a visual treatment for completed actions in the project view: same `.gtd-item--completed` modifier we discussed (strikethrough + muted color), consistent with the current MVP pattern
- For actions NOT linked to a project, completed items are simply gone from the Next Actions view. This is correct for GTD -- once done, it's done.

### 3. Existing Todo Data Migration
Migrating pending todos to inbox items makes sense. They'll need to go through the clarify flow to get contexts and project assignments. Completed todos to a completed archive is fine, or we could just drop them -- the MVP has few users. Either way, this is a database concern more than a CSS one. The frontend just needs to handle the new URL structure cleanly.

### 4. Quick Capture from Every Page
**Yes, I support this**, and it has significant CSS implications that I want to flag:

The persistent capture input would live in the header area of `base_gtd.html` (Carson and I agreed on a template hierarchy: `base.html` -> `base_auth.html` / `base_gtd.html`). Every authenticated page inherits it.

CSS considerations:
- The header will now contain: **brand/title + quick capture input + nav links + logout**. That's a lot of elements to arrange. On desktop, this can be a flexbox row. On mobile, the capture input should probably stack below the brand and above the content.
- The capture input needs to be visually distinct from the page content -- it's a global action, not part of the current view. A subtle background difference or a separator border would help.
- The input should be compact but usable. Label can be visually hidden (the placeholder "Capture..." plus the input's position in the header makes its purpose clear visually), but it MUST have a `<label>` for screen readers.
- When the user is ON the inbox page, we have two capture inputs: the persistent header one and the inbox page's own add form. We should either hide the page-level input on the inbox page (since the header one suffices) or make the header input redirect/scroll to the inbox. Otherwise it's confusing which one to use. **I'd recommend: on the inbox page, the header capture input IS the primary capture input, and there's no second one in the page body.**

### Frontend-Specific Feedback on User Stories

**US-GTD-1 (Capture)**: "Inbox items show in a list ordered by capture time (newest first)" -- I'd push back slightly. GTD processing works top-to-bottom, and oldest-first means you process the thing that's been sitting longest. Newest-first means recent captures push older items down, and the inbox never gets fully processed. **Recommendation: oldest first**, which is also the natural list order (append new items at the bottom).

**US-GTD-2 (Clarify)**: The acceptance criteria mention "process view for a single item" at `/inbox/{id}/clarify` -- this aligns with what Heydon and Carson converged on. From a CSS perspective, this is a form page. The conditional fields (context selector appears when "Next Action" is chosen, "waiting on" field appears when "Waiting For" is chosen) can use CSS `:has()` for progressive disclosure with zero JavaScript. The fallback (no `:has()` support) is all fields visible, which is functional.

**US-GTD-3 (Next Actions)**: "Context filter is a set of links/buttons, not a dropdown (scannable)" -- perfect. This maps to a horizontal list of filter links within the page, `hx-boost`ed. CSS is simple: flex-wrap row of styled links, with `aria-current="page"` on the active filter. The grouped-by-context default view uses sticky section headers (I discussed this with Scott).

**US-GTD-8 (Navigation)**: The acceptance criterion "Quick capture input is available on every authenticated page" needs the duplicate-input consideration I flagged above. Also, the dashboard summary (inbox count, stalled projects, next actions count, waiting for count) is a new CSS component -- a grid of stat cards. Simple to style but worth noting as a net-new component.

## Navigation Layout -- Open Question

You should be aware there's a team discussion happening about whether the GTD navigation should be a sidebar (Steve Schoger's direction) or a header nav bar (Heydon's recommendation for accessibility). I've asked Steve Krug for usability input. This doesn't block your product discovery -- the navigation destinations are the same either way. But the layout choice affects template structure and CSS architecture significantly. Your input on whether this feels like a "power tool with sidebar" or a "focused app with header nav" would help the team converge.
